title: Open Workshop: Trade Coordination, Warranties, Vertical Integration, and Finish Details
summary: This open workshop session covered a range of participant-submitted challenges including managing slow or unreliable subcontractors (particularly cabinet makers and trim carpenters), structuring warranty programs for homeowners, and the pros and cons of vertical integration by bringing trades in-house. Additional topics included diagnosing a recurring moisture issue under flooring and executing drywall return windows and doors correctly.

teachings.concept: Root Cause Discovery Before Escalating Trade Issues
teachings.explanation: When a subcontractor is behind or unresponsive, the first step is to understand why rather than immediately escalating or replacing them. There may be solvable problems like staffing, scheduling overload, or administrative bottlenecks that the builder can help resolve collaboratively.
teachings.quote: We usually just recognize that like, Hey, we know you're stuck. What's going on? Like fill us in as your schedule too many projects. Do you have people who are sick? Like we try to actually forget that it's our project and really try to figure out like what the problem is because there may be a way that we can help solve that problem.

teachings.concept: Written Day-by-Day Commitment Schedules with Trades
teachings.explanation: Rather than getting vague commitments from trades, sit down with them and map out every single day for the next two weeks. This forces clarity, creates accountability, and gives both parties something concrete to reference.
teachings.quote: I literally sit down with them and say, okay, look, let's work out a plan. And I don't just mean like, Oh, I'll be here Tuesday. I'm more, I want to know every day for the next two weeks, what we're doing at this house or what we have going on. And I sit with them. Like I do with my kids.

teachings.explanation: Structuring your trade teams so that they are interdependent creates natural accountability. When one trade needs another to finish before they can start, they will organically apply pressure and help keep the schedule moving.
teachings.quote: I actually have like the other trades put the pressure on the trim carpenter because they've got to get finished. Wood floor guys will put the pressure on the trim guy.

teachings.concept: Parceling Work Across Multiple Trades When Necessary
teachings.explanation: When a single trade cannot complete a job on schedule, it is acceptable and sometimes necessary to bring in a second trade to complete the remaining scope. This pragmatic approach prioritizes project completion over loyalty to one vendor.
teachings.quote: We would tell the trim carpenter or painter, trim carpenter, you just finished these things. We're going to contract these other things with this other trim carpenter. So we've parceled homes apart too, to where it is what it is.

teachings.concept: Proactive Maintenance Program Setup at Move-In
teachings.explanation: Rather than waiting for warranty issues to arise, immediately help homeowners set up maintenance programs for key systems (HVAC, gutters, roof, plumbing) when they move in. This prevents problems before they start and reduces warranty disputes.
teachings.quote: As soon as they move in, we're lining, we're helping them line up HVAC, you know, where the HVAC company comes out four times a year, checks their filters, checks the drain lines. So that's our immediate move because it alleviates all of this warranty conversation later.

teachings.concept: Third-Party Warranty Products as a Builder Option
teachings.explanation: Builders can purchase outside warranty products that define specific standards for defects and transfer responsibility off the builder's plate. These products provide clear, objective criteria (e.g., a crack you can fit a quarter into vs. a penny) and give homeowners confidence.
teachings.quote: If the crack in the concrete, if you can stick a quarter in it, then that's a problem. If you stick a penny in it, then that's just the way concrete works. So it lists all of these details out.

teachings.concept: Vertical Integration of Trades: Upside and Risks
teachings.explanation: Bringing certain trades in-house (such as cabinet installation) can improve schedule control and quality consistency, but requires investment in payroll, equipment, and leadership. The key to making it work is having a trusted person running that division with skin in the game.
teachings.quote: You have to have somebody that can also run that side as well, that you can trust that's in it and has maybe some skin in the game. That's going to stay for the long haul because if not, I think it's going to be hard to keep it going.

teachings.explanation: Rather than imposing a schedule on a trade, ask them to define how long their work will take and then hold them to their own commitment. Trades are more likely to honor timelines they set themselves, and this creates shared ownership of the schedule.
teachings.quote: I said, okay, I need to know how many days it's going to take you to build days, weeks, months, however long it is, to build the cabinets. And I need to know exactly what's the timeframe for installation. And then I let them set that for me and I put it into my construction schedule.

actionable_advice: When a trade is behind, sit down with them face-to-face and map out a day-by-day plan for the next two weeks, writing it out together so both parties are clear on commitments.
Dig past the first excuse a trade gives you — the real problem is usually something solvable, like a staffing gap or administrative issue you can help them resolve.
If a trade cannot complete a job, consider parceling the remaining scope to a second trade rather than waiting indefinitely.
As soon as homeowners move in, proactively help them set up maintenance programs for HVAC, gutters, roof, plumbing, and home automation systems.
Consider budgeting a warranty cost code (e.g., $10,000 per home) to account for the reality that builders will inevitably spend money going back to fix things.
Explore purchasing a third-party warranty product to define objective standards for defect coverage and reduce disputes with homeowners.
If considering in-house trade integration, ensure you have a trusted leader with skin in the game to run that division before committing.
For drywall return windows, do mock-ups with your trim carpenter and sheet rocker together before committing to a full execution approach.
For a jam extension on drywall return windows, kerf a line into the jam so the sheet rock bead can return cleanly into it.
Build float days into your construction schedule before and after a trade's committed start date to absorb minor schedule slippage.
For unresolved moisture issues under flooring, bring in a dedicated moisture specialist to take moisture readings and assess slab conditions before re-laying floors.
client_challenges: A participant building her own home is struggling to get her cabinet maker (located 90 minutes away) to show up consistently and complete the work, while other trades wait on him to finish.
A participant asked how to structure and communicate warranties to homeowners, including when equipment warranties start and how to handle borderline cases of homeowner versus builder responsibility.
Several participants struggle to find reliable cabinet makers, with small local shops being inconsistent in scheduling and delivery.
A participant asked whether it makes sense to vertically integrate certain trades like cabinet making into the building company to solve reliability issues.

A participant is dealing with a recurring moisture problem in a client's master bedroom — water is wicking up through layers of old saltillo tile and sub-floor, causing vinyl plank to bubble repeatedly.
A participant is preparing to execute drywall return windows and doors on a project with pre-finished black windows and wants to ensure the process is done correctly from the start.
notable_quotes: On diagnosing trade delays: 'We try to actually forget that it's our project and really try to figure out like what the problem is because there may be a way that we can help solve that problem.'
On creating trade accountability: 'I sit with them. Like I do with my kids. Like I sit with them and I write it out with them.'
On the inevitability of warranty costs: 'Any given project, you know, I'll end up spending at least $10,000 on a home that I've gone back to fix something.'

On the value of getting stuck and being forced to make a move: 'I had no idea that this other move was the person to work with, but it's because I was stuck. And so it's interesting to see how all the dots are connected when you're in a corner and you have to make a move.'

On the importance of buy-in, drawing on a medical analogy: 'Her entire day is based on getting buy-in from someone who is willing to go through chemo and radiation and surgeries... like her entire day, like you said, like it's just getting the buy-in.'
On creating a safe zone with trades: 'No judgment here, like I don't play the blame game. I just have to plan and once they know there's a safe zone, then you can get the info.'
